resolver_decisions.evidence: Table 3 (Mixed-effects regressions overall, n=15,022) reports regression coefficients (b), standard errors (SE), and p-values for screen time outcome across two models. Model 1 reports: Income (≥50K-<100K): b = −0.60, SE = 0.08, p < 0.001; Income (>100K): b = −1.20, SE = 0.08, p < 0.001; Race (Black): b = 1.32, SE = 0.09, p < 0.001; Race (Asian): b = −0.52, SE = 0.19, p = 0.005; Race (Mixed/Other): b = 0.18, SE = 0.08, p = 0.019. Model 2 includes interaction terms with corresponding coefficients, e.g., Income (≥50K-<100K) × Race (Black): b = 0.59, SE = 0.21, p = 0.004; Income (>100K) × Race (Black): b = 0.74, SE = 0.25, p = 0.003.
resolver_decisions.rationale: Per B8, any traditional regression coefficient for the SM variable reported in any analysis (main, supplementary, sensitivity) satisfies FT_IC4 = MET. The paper reports multiple regression coefficients (β values) with SEs and p-values in the main text table (Table 3), clearly documenting the association between household income and children's screen time. Both C1 and C2 were unable to extract from the abstract alone, but the full PDF contains explicit numeric values. C2's determination of NOT_MET was incorrect because it relied on incomplete review of paper content; the numeric coefficients are plainly present in the results section.
